title: Anger at Statues, Monuments, and Whose Story
dek: *When you walk past a monument and feel your chest tighten, you're not overreacting. You're reading stone and bronze for what they've always been: declarations of power, memory made permanent, and a choice about whose suffering gets remembered and whose gets paved over. That fury is information.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger at statues and monuments signals a collision between inherited narratives and present-day values, revealing whose stories have been centered. This rage often carries unprocessed grief about exclusion, erasure, and the violence embedded in public memory. Learning to work with that anger consciously can transform it from reactive destruction into deliberate reclamation of shared space.

Have you ever stood in front of a statue and felt something twist in your stomach, a heat rising that you couldn't quite name? Maybe it was a Confederate general on horseback in a town square, or a conquistador planted at the entrance to a park where your grandmother used to walk. Maybe it was a plaque that sanitized atrocity into "exploration" or "settlement." The anger that arrives in those moments isn't irrational. It's your body recognizing a story that was never yours, imposed as if it were universal truth. Monuments don't just commemorate. They claim. They say: this version of events is the one that matters. This person's valor outweighs that person's dispossession. This sacrifice is noble; that one is invisible. When communities erupt in fury over statues, it's not about hating history. It's about refusing to let a single, often violent story stand unchallenged in the center of public life. ## The Weight of Stone and What It Declares A monument occupies space. It interrupts your walk, your commute, your daily geography. It doesn't ask permission. It announces itself as permanent, as settled fact. In our experience, people don't get angry at abstract symbols. They get angry when those symbols sit in the middle of their lived reality, demanding reverence for a past that harmed them or people who look like them. Psychologist Harriet Lerner writes about anger as a signal that something important is at stake, often a question of fairness or respect. When a bronze figure looms over a plaza, it's making a claim about whose contributions deserve public honor. If your ancestors were enslaved by the man on that pedestal, or driven from their land by the policies he championed, that statue isn't neutral decoration. It's an ongoing assertion that his story matters more than theirs. The anger that rises isn't just about the past. It's about now. It's about walking your child past a monument to someone who would have denied your family's humanity. It's about city budgets that maintain these figures while underfunding schools in your neighborhood. The stone carries the weight of priorities made visible. ## Whose Memory, Whose Grief Monuments are selective. They choose memory, deciding what gets frozen in marble and what dissolves into footnotes. Across the articles we've published on this site, we've returned again and again to the idea that anger often masks grief. When you're furious at a statue, part of what you're grieving is the absence of counter-monuments, the missing tributes to those who resisted, who survived, who built communities in the ruins left by the celebrated. Author and activist Soraya Chemaly has pointed out that women's anger is frequently dismissed as hysteria or overreaction, a pattern that extends to any marginalized group challenging official narratives. The anger at monuments gets coded as vandalism, as disrespect for history, as mob rule. But what if it's actually a demand for a fuller accounting? What if tearing down a statue is less about erasing the past and more about refusing to let one chapter dominate the entire library? Your body knows when a story is incomplete. You feel it as tension in your shoulders when you pass certain intersections, a clench in your jaw when you read the plaque that calls a massacre a "battle." That physical response is grief announcing itself through rage. Someone decided this version was the only one worth preserving in bronze. Someone decided your people's pain wasn't monumental. ## The Body's Reaction to Public Memory Stand in front of a contested monument and pay attention. Your heart rate might spike. Your breathing might shallow. Your hands might curl into fists without your conscious intention. This isn't melodrama. This is your nervous system reading the environment for threat, and a statue that glorifies your historical oppressor registers as threat. The CDC's guidance on stress and the body explains how chronic exposure to symbols of historical trauma can activate the same physiological pathways as direct danger. You're not imagining the tightness in your chest. The monument is a material reminder that the systems which harmed your community still shape the landscape, still decide what's worth honoring. In my own practice, I've worked with people who avoid entire neighborhoods because of the monuments there. One woman described taking a ten-minute detour every morning to skip a particular statue, not because she was being dramatic, but because starting her day with that visual assertion of her insignificance made her feel physically ill. Her anger wasn't abstract. It was embodied, visceral, tied to the concrete geography of her city. When communities organize to remove or recontextualize monuments, they're not erasing history. They're trying to stop the daily re-traumatization of walking past a celebration of their ancestors' suffering. The anger is protective. It says: I will not normalize this. I will not pretend this is fine. ## The Difference Between Destroying and Reclaiming There's a spectrum between toppling a statue in the street and moving it to a museum with historical context. Both can be acts of reclamation, depending on the community's needs and the monument's meaning. The rage people feel isn't always calling for destruction. Sometimes it's calling for honesty. Buddhist teacher Thich Nhat Hanh spoke about anger as a kind of suffering that needs to be held with compassion, not acted out blindly. That doesn't mean suppressing justified fury at monuments to oppression. It means distinguishing between reactive destruction and conscious transformation. When a statue comes down in a moment of collective uprising, that's one kind of processing. When a community votes to relocate it, add counter-monuments, or create new installations that tell suppressed stories, that's another. Both can be valid. The key is whether the anger gets channeled into something that serves the community's healing or just vents without changing the underlying power dynamics. A removed statue that gets replaced with nothing leaves a void. A removed statue that makes space for a memorial to those who resisted, who survived, who thrived despite everything, that's reclamation. Your anger at monuments can become a compass. It points you toward the stories that need strengthening, the histories that deserve equal public space, the narratives that have been waiting in archives and oral traditions for their turn in bronze. ## A Practice: Reading the Landscape Next time you're near a monument that provokes a reaction, try this. It takes less than five minutes and can shift your relationship with the anger. Stand at a comfortable distance. Don't force yourself closer if it feels unsafe. Notice what your body is doing. Where do you feel the anger? Chest, throat, hands, stomach? Just note it without trying to change it. Now ask yourself three questions. First: What story is this monument telling? Be specific. Not "history," but whose version, whose valor, whose victory. Second: What story is missing from this spot? Who lived here before the monument? Who was harmed by the person or event being celebrated? Third: If you could add something to this landscape, what would it be? A counter-monument, a plaque with fuller context, a garden dedicated to those erased? You don't have to act on any of this immediately. The practice is in training yourself to read public memory critically, to let your anger be a guide rather than just a burden. Write down your answers if that helps. Share them with people who understand. Let the rage sharpen your vision instead of clouding it. ## When Anger Becomes Organizing Individual fury at a statue is one thing. Collective anger that mobilizes into action is another. Over the years I've seen communities transform rage into campaigns for historical markers, for renaming streets, for installing new monuments that reflect a broader truth. That process isn't always smooth. There are public meetings where people yell, city councils that stall, opponents who frame any change as attack on heritage. But the anger, when it's metabolized into organizing, becomes generative. It builds coalitions. It surfaces buried archives. It trains a new generation to question whose stories get carved in stone and whose get left to weather away. The tools in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) can help here, especially the sections on sustaining energy through long campaigns and managing the rage that comes when institutions resist change. Some people find that adaptogens like [Host Defense Stamets 7 (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B002WIVHNM?tag=spankyspinola-20) help them stay grounded during sustained organizing work, supporting the body's resilience when anger needs to be a marathon rather than a sprint. Others use nervines like [Pure Encapsulations Pure Tranquility Liquid](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008EKVG3I?tag=spankyspinola-20) to soothe the overstimulation that comes from constant exposure to triggering symbols and the meetings where their validity is debated. The point isn't to dampen your anger. It's to resource yourself so you can keep showing up, keep speaking, keep pushing for the landscape to reflect a truer, more inclusive memory. ## The Monument You Carry Here's the thing: you don't need permission from a city council to start honoring the stories that matter to you. While you're working on changing public monuments, you can also tend the internal ones. What memories do you carry about resilience, about your people's survival, about beauty created in the margins? On [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), there's a whole thread about personal altars and memory-keeping as acts of resistance. When the public landscape refuses to reflect your truth, your private practice becomes even more vital. Light a candle for ancestors who were never memorialized. Tell their stories to your children. Write their names where you'll see them daily. This isn't a substitute for demanding changes to shared public space. It's a parallel practice. You can hold both: the rage at what's present in the town square and the devotion to what you're preserving in your home. Sometimes the flower essence [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Oak - Restore Endurance - Natural Homeopathic Flo](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005P0UPGY?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support that kind of dual endurance, the capacity to keep fighting externally while staying rooted internally. Your anger at monuments is asking you to become a different kind of monument yourself. Not stone, but living memory. Not fixed, but adapting, telling, retelling, ensuring that the stories you carry don't get paved over by someone else's version of importance. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Isn't removing statues erasing history? No. History exists in archives, books, curricula, museums, and living memory. A statue in a place of honor is a statement about values, not a neutral record. Moving it to a museum with full context, or replacing it with a monument that tells a more complete story, is expanding history, not erasing it. The anger at monuments is often anger that one narrow version has been presented as the whole truth. ### What if the monument honors someone who did both good and harm? Most historical figures are complicated. The question is whether the public monument acknowledges that complexity or presents them as unambiguous heroes. Adding context, counter-monuments, or interpretive plaques can hold the nuance. Your anger might be less about the person and more about the sanitized story. Communities can decide to keep a statue while adding information about the harm that person caused, creating a fuller reckoning rather than simple removal. ### How do I talk to people who think I'm overreacting? Start with your body. Explain that this isn't abstract for you, that walking past certain monuments has a physical impact, that the anger is a response to real harm, historical and ongoing. Use specific examples: "This statue celebrates someone who signed the order to remove my ancestors from their land" is harder to dismiss than "I just don't like it." You're not required to convince everyone. Sometimes the best you can do is state your truth clearly and find the people who already understand. [The assessments hub](/assessments) has tools for identifying when a conversation is actually changing someone's mind versus when you're just exhausting yourself. ## The Landscape Ahead Statues will keep coming down. New monuments will rise. The arguments will continue, because they're not really about bronze and marble. They're about who gets to shape collective memory, whose pain is acknowledged, whose triumph is celebrated. Your anger at monuments is part of a much larger conversation that's been happening for generations and will continue long after any individual statue is relocated or replaced. That doesn't make your fury any less valid or urgent. It situates it in a lineage of people who refused to accept the landscape as given, who insisted that public space reflect a fuller truth. The next time you feel that twist in your stomach in front of a monument, let it teach you. Let it show you what stories you're hungry for, what histories you want strengthened, what kind of public memory would let you walk through your city without your body registering threat. Then find one small way to act on that knowledge. Attend a city council meeting. Support an artist creating counter-monuments. Tell the suppressed story to someone who's never heard it. Your anger knows where it wants to go. The question is whether you'll trust it enough to follow. ## Keep reading - [Sandwich Generation Anger: The Math Doesn't Work](/article/sandwich-generation-anger-the-math-doesnt-work) - [Anger at Other Parents at the Playground](/article/anger-at-other-parents-at-the-playground) - [Anger When Your Child Is the One Bullying](/article/anger-when-your-child-is-the-one-bullying) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on anger at statues, monuments, and whose story, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
